32.6 Final recommendations

  • Treat managed artifacts as immutable release units.
  • Separate environment strategy from project convenience; enforce policy centrally.
  • Adopt a layered governance model: preventive controls, detective controls, corrective runbooks.
  • Institutionalize ALM as a product capability, not a one-time setup.
  • Continuously improve through metrics, incident learnings, and template hardening.

33. Scenario playbooks

33.1 Emergency hotfix in production

  • Objective: Use managed patch flow, pre-backup, minimal scope import, and immediate post-validation.
  • Entry criteria: Change ticket approved, owner assigned, communication plan prepared.
  • Execution steps: 1. Validate prerequisites and backups. 2. Execute deployment or governance action in controlled window. 3. Run smoke tests and compliance checks. 4. Publish completion summary with evidence links.
  • Exit criteria: Target KPI stable for 24 hours and no high severity incidents.

33.2 Cross-team shared component release

  • Objective: Version shared solution first, then dependent solutions in defined order.
  • Entry criteria: Change ticket approved, owner assigned, communication plan prepared.
  • Execution steps: 1. Validate prerequisites and backups. 2. Execute deployment or governance action in controlled window. 3. Run smoke tests and compliance checks. 4. Publish completion summary with evidence links.
  • Exit criteria: Target KPI stable for 24 hours and no high severity incidents.

33.3 Environment regional migration

  • Objective: Create target region env, deploy managed solution, migrate data, cut over with freeze window.
  • Entry criteria: Change ticket approved, owner assigned, communication plan prepared.
  • Execution steps: 1. Validate prerequisites and backups. 2. Execute deployment or governance action in controlled window. 3. Run smoke tests and compliance checks. 4. Publish completion summary with evidence links.
  • Exit criteria: Target KPI stable for 24 hours and no high severity incidents.

33.4 DLP policy tightening program

  • Objective: Classify connector usage, notify owners, remediate alternatives, enforce block in phases.
  • Entry criteria: Change ticket approved, owner assigned, communication plan prepared.
  • Execution steps: 1. Validate prerequisites and backups. 2. Execute deployment or governance action in controlled window. 3. Run smoke tests and compliance checks. 4. Publish completion summary with evidence links.
  • Exit criteria: Target KPI stable for 24 hours and no high severity incidents.

33.5 Capacity crisis response

  • Objective: Stop non-essential jobs, archive data, purchase add-on, execute optimization backlog.
  • Entry criteria: Change ticket approved, owner assigned, communication plan prepared.
  • Execution steps: 1. Validate prerequisites and backups. 2. Execute deployment or governance action in controlled window. 3. Run smoke tests and compliance checks. 4. Publish completion summary with evidence links.
  • Exit criteria: Target KPI stable for 24 hours and no high severity incidents.

34. Decision records template

# ADR-PP-XXX: <Decision Title>
## Context
- Business driver:
- Technical constraints:
- Compliance constraints:
## Decision
- Chosen option:
- Scope and boundaries:
## Consequences
- Positive outcomes:
- Trade-offs:
## Operationalization
- Pipeline updates:
- Governance controls:
- Rollback approach:

35. Environment tier baseline matrix

Control Dev Test UAT Prod
Managed environment Optional Yes Yes Yes
Maker role breadth Broad Limited Limited Minimal
DLP strictness Moderate High High Highest
Backup frequency Weekly Daily Daily Daily + retention policy
Deployment source Manual or CI CI only CI only CI only with approvals
Monitoring depth Basic Standard Standard Enhanced with on-call

36. Closing guidance

  • ALM maturity is not measured by tool adoption alone; it is measured by repeatability, recoverability, and governance evidence.
  • Environment strategy must be explicit and policy-backed, not tribal knowledge.
  • The fastest teams in production are those with the strongest pre-deployment controls and the clearest rollback paths.

connectors-mcp-flows.md

tech/agents/power-platform-advisor/knowledge/connectors-mcp-flows.md

Power Platform Advisor Knowledge: Custom Connectors, MCP, and Agent Flows

Author: Power Platform Advisor Knowledge Base Last Updated (UTC): 2026-03-02 13:02:36 Repository Path: tech/agents/power-platform-advisor/knowledge/connectors-mcp-flows.md Scope: Microsoft Power Platform custom connectors, Model Context Protocol (MCP), and Power Automate integration for Copilot Studio agents.

How to Use This Reference

  • Use this as an implementation guide when designing integration surfaces for Copilot Studio and Power Platform agents.
  • Treat examples as production-oriented templates; adapt security, naming, and environment specifics.
  • For orchestration behavior, prioritize clear descriptions because LLM routing depends heavily on semantic metadata.
  • For ALM, keep connector specs, flow contracts, and topic YAML in source control with versioned change notes.

Table of Contents

    1. Custom Connectors Deep Reference
    1. MCP Deep Reference
    1. Power Automate Integration (Agent Flows) Deep Reference
    1. Architecture Patterns
    1. Governance, Security, and Operations
    1. Troubleshooting Playbooks
    1. Implementation Checklists
    1. Appendix: Reusable Snippets
    1. Appendix: Validation and Review Rubric
    1. Appendix: Glossary

1) Custom Connectors Deep Reference

1.1 What custom connectors are

A custom connector is a Power Platform abstraction over an HTTP-based API, defined by an OpenAPI (Swagger/OpenAPI) contract plus Power Platform extensions. It converts REST operations into low-code actions and triggers that can be consumed by:

  • Power Automate flows
  • Power Apps apps
  • Copilot Studio tools and agent actions
  • Logic-like automation patterns when wrapped in flows

A custom connector is the right integration mechanism when you need governance, reusable metadata, and first-class platform lifecycle support.

1.2 When to build a custom connector

Scenario Build Custom Connector? Why
Internal API reused by multiple makers Yes Centralized contract, auth reuse, DLP governance
One-off call for a single topic Maybe HTTP action may be faster initially, connector better for scale
Need trigger semantics from webhook/polling endpoint Yes Connector trigger model supports event or polling patterns
Need rich parameter metadata and dynamic dropdowns Yes x-ms-* extensions improve maker UX and LLM routing
Need immediate prototype with unknown API stability No initially Use HTTP action first, productize later as connector
Need marketplace discoverability Yes Certification allows broader availability

1.3 OpenAPI authoring foundations: Swagger 2.0 vs OpenAPI 3.0

Capability Swagger 2.0 OpenAPI 3.0 Implementation Notes
Body schema location parameters: in: body requestBody Conversion may be needed when migrating
Content negotiation Limited First-class content object 3.0 is clearer for multi-content APIs
Components reuse definitions components/schemas 3.0 improves composition clarity
Examples Basic Richer examples per media type Better for maker understanding
Callbacks/webhooks modeling Minimal Native patterns Useful for trigger documentation
Power Platform compatibility Strong Strong (growing) Validate with paconn before import

Minimum required fields for import success generally include:

  • openapi or swagger version
  • info.title and info.version
  • host and basePath (Swagger 2.0) or servers (OpenAPI 3.0)
  • At least one operation under paths
  • Response schema for each operation (strongly recommended even if not strictly required)

1.4 Operation descriptions for LLM routing and maker usability

For agent and copilots, operation semantics are critical. Write operation descriptions with this pattern:

  1. What it does in one sentence.
  2. When it should be used.
  3. Required key inputs and constraints.
  4. High-level output semantics.
  5. Any preconditions or side effects.

Poor descriptions degrade tool selection quality in generative orchestration.

1.5 Power Platform OpenAPI extensions (x-ms-*)

Extension Purpose Typical Location Example
x-ms-summary Friendly operation/parameter label Operation, parameter x-ms-summary: Get customer profile
x-ms-visibility Hide or mark fields advanced/internal Parameter, schema property x-ms-visibility: advanced
x-ms-dynamic-values Populate dropdown values from another operation Parameter Country list from metadata endpoint
x-ms-trigger Declare operation as trigger metadata Operation single/batch trigger behavior

Additional high-impact extensions often used in mature connectors:

  • x-ms-dynamic-schema for runtime schema shape resolution
  • x-ms-dynamic-properties for dynamic object properties
  • x-ms-capabilities for pagination, chunking, and advanced behaviors
  • x-ms-pageable for pageable actions where applicable

1.6 Authentication patterns

OAuth 2.0: Authorization Code

  • Best for delegated user context scenarios
  • Requires authorization URL, token URL, client ID/secret, scopes
  • Scope naming must exactly match provider expectations
  • Redirect URI must be correctly registered for Power Platform

OAuth 2.0: Client Credentials

  • Best for service-to-service or daemon-style operations
  • No interactive user consent per invocation
  • Requires tenant-approved app permissions and admin consent
  • Ensure token audience matches downstream API resource

API Key

  • Header-based key (preferred over query when possible)
  • Query-key can be used for legacy APIs but leaks into logs more easily
  • Rotate keys via secret management and connection updates

Basic Authentication

  • Only for APIs that cannot modernize
  • Use TLS always
  • Prefer connector-level secret management, never hard-coded credentials in specs

No Authentication

  • Use only for public data endpoints
  • Avoid exposing high-value internal APIs without auth even in dev

1.7 DLP classification and HTTP connector controls

DLP Group Meaning Impact on Design
Business Trusted enterprise connectors Can combine with other Business connectors
Non-business Consumer/untrusted tools Isolated from Business in same policy scope
Blocked Prohibited connectors Cannot be used in target environments

DLP policy architecture notes:

  • HTTP connector controls are often stricter because raw HTTP can bypass governance intent.
  • If HTTP is blocked, custom connector may still be allowed if reviewed and classified correctly.
  • Build policy-aware integration plans for Dev, Test, and Prod separately.

1.8 Connector certification

Certification is the process to validate connector quality, security, reliability, and documentation for broader availability.

Benefits include:

  • Trust and discoverability
  • Standardized support expectations
  • Reuse across teams/tenants (depending on publication path)
  • Better lifecycle confidence for platform admins

Typical requirements include API stability, production-grade error handling, legal/compliance artifacts, branding, and test readiness.

1.9 Triggers vs actions

Operation Type Use Case Design Guidance
Action Request-response operation Define deterministic inputs/outputs and error schema
Webhook Trigger Push events from source API Validate handshake and secure callback endpoint
Polling Trigger Pull for new data periodically Support state/watermark and pagination

Trigger design requirements:

  • Define stable event identity for deduplication
  • Include event timestamp and source correlation ID
  • Ensure payload schema versioning strategy
  • Support replays where feasible for resilience

1.10 Pagination and x-ms-capabilities

Server-driven pagination should expose clear continuation tokens or next links. When available, annotate capabilities to improve connector runtime behavior.

Key guidance:

  • Return deterministic ordering for pageable endpoints
  • Include nextLink or token in response schema
  • Define sensible default and maximum page sizes
  • Document throttling and retry behavior for high-volume pulls

1.11 Custom code (C# script transforms)

Custom code is useful when API contract mismatches cannot be solved by OpenAPI modeling alone.

Use custom code for:

  • Header/body transformations
  • Dynamic signature computation
  • Protocol quirks in legacy APIs
  • Data shape normalization when upstream responses are inconsistent

Avoid custom code when simple spec updates can solve the problem; custom code increases maintenance burden.

1.12 Creation workflow (end-to-end)

  1. Author OpenAPI spec with complete operation metadata
  2. Add Power Platform extensions for UX and runtime behavior
  3. Validate schema and connector package locally
  4. Create connector in portal or via paconn CLI
  5. Configure authentication and connection parameters
  6. Test operations with representative payloads and error conditions
  7. Share with security/DLP owners for policy classification
  8. Promote through environments with ALM controls
  9. Publish documentation and sample flow/topic usage
  10. Monitor connector usage, failures, and auth refresh behavior

1.13 paconn CLI operations

Common commands (examples):

paconn validate --api-def apiDefinition.swagger.json --api-prop apiProperties.json
paconn create --api-def apiDefinition.swagger.json --api-prop apiProperties.json --icon icon.png
paconn update --api-def apiDefinition.swagger.json --api-prop apiProperties.json --icon icon.png --connector-id <id>

Operational notes:

  • Run validate in CI before update/create.
  • Keep connector IDs and environment mappings in deployment metadata.
  • Version info.version and changelog together.

1.14 Common custom connector gotchas

  1. Silent feature ignoring when unsupported extension syntax is used
  2. OAuth scope mismatch between auth configuration and token audience
  3. Array-heavy responses causing usability/performance issues in maker experiences
  4. Binary upload/download not modeled with correct content types
  5. Missing operation summaries causing poor discoverability
  6. Overly generic parameter names reducing AI routing precision
  7. Undocumented error schemas forcing fragile downstream parsing
  8. Dynamic values operation requiring auth context not available at design time
  9. Incorrect host/basePath/server values during environment promotion
  10. Pagination tokens returned but not mapped in schema definitions

1.15 OpenAPI snippet (Swagger 2.0 with Power Platform extensions)

swagger: "2.0"
info:
  title: Contoso Customer API
  version: "1.2.0"
host: api.contoso.com
basePath: /v1
schemes:
  - https
consumes:
  - application/json
produces:
  - application/json
paths:
  /customers/{customerId}:
    get:
      operationId: GetCustomer
      summary: Get customer profile
      description: Retrieves a customer profile by ID for read-only agent lookup scenarios.
      x-ms-summary: Get customer profile
      parameters:
        - name: customerId
          in: path
          required: true
          type: string
          description: Unique customer identifier
      responses:
        "200":
          description: Success
          schema:
            $ref: "#/definitions/Customer"
        default:
          description: Error
          schema:
            $ref: "#/definitions/ErrorResponse"
  /events/new-customer:
    get:
      operationId: OnNewCustomer
      summary: Trigger when customer created
      x-ms-trigger: single
      x-ms-visibility: important
      responses:
        "200":
          description: Event payload
          schema:
            type: object
            properties:
              eventId:
                type: string
              customerId:
                type: string
definitions:
  Customer:
    type: object
    properties:
      id:
        type: string
      fullName:
        type: string
      segment:
        type: string
  ErrorResponse:
    type: object
    properties:
      code:
        type: string
      message:
        type: string

1.16 OpenAPI 3.0 snippet with improved requestBody and responses

openapi: 3.0.3
info:
  title: Contoso Orders API
  version: 2.0.0
servers:
  - url: https://orders.contoso.com/api
paths:
  /orders:
    post:
      operationId: CreateOrder
      summary: Create order
      description: Creates a new order for a validated customer.
      x-ms-summary: Create order
      requestBody:
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              $ref: "#/components/schemas/CreateOrderRequest"
      responses:
        "201":
          description: Created
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/CreateOrderResponse"
        "400":
          description: Bad request
          content:
            application/json:
              schema:
                $ref: "#/components/schemas/ErrorResponse"
components:
  schemas:
    CreateOrderRequest:
      type: object
      required: [customerId, items]
      properties:
        customerId:
          type: string
        items:
          type: array
          items:
            type: object
            required: [sku, quantity]
            properties:
              sku: { type: string }
              quantity: { type: integer, minimum: 1 }
    CreateOrderResponse:
      type: object
      properties:
        orderId: { type: string }
        status: { type: string }
    ErrorResponse:
      type: object
      properties:
        code: { type: string }
        message: { type: string }

2) MCP (Model Context Protocol) Deep Reference

2.1 What MCP is

MCP is a protocol for exposing tools and resources to AI systems through a standardized interface. In Copilot Studio, MCP servers extend an agent with externally managed capabilities without hardcoding each action in the agent.

MCP enables:

  • Tool discoverability by schema
  • Runtime evolution as server-side tools change
  • Centralized capability management across multiple agents
  • Cleaner separation between orchestration and execution

2.2 MCP in Copilot Studio lifecycle

  1. Register MCP server in agent settings.
  2. Discover tools/resources from server metadata.
  3. Enable generative orchestration so the LLM can select tools.
  4. Validate tool calling behavior with test utterances.
  5. Monitor failures and schema drift over time.

2.3 Tool definitions: required semantic fields

Field Why it matters Guidance
Name Primary retrieval token Use explicit verb+noun, e.g., getCustomerInvoice
Description Main routing signal Include intent, constraints, and exclusion criteria
Input schema Parameter extraction Use strict types and clear required fields
Output schema Downstream composition Return stable object shape with status + data + diagnostics
Error schema Recovery logic Include machine-readable error code and retry hint

2.4 Semantic routing for tool selection

LLM tool selection quality is proportional to metadata quality.

Routing optimization guidelines:

  • Avoid ambiguous tool names such as process or execute
  • Include domain terms users naturally say
  • Explicitly state when not to use a tool
  • Keep descriptions concise but specific
  • Prefer stable parameter names aligned to conversational language

2.5 Agent-to-MCP binding patterns

Pattern Description Best For
Single server, many tools One MCP endpoint exposes broad domain Unified platform APIs with shared auth
Domain servers Separate MCP by business domain Team ownership boundaries
Capability tiering Read-only and write-capable servers separated Risk reduction and approval gating
Regional servers Region-local MCP endpoints Data residency and latency requirements

2.6 Available MCP server ecosystem patterns

Common server categories seen in enterprise ecosystems:

  • Dataverse data and metadata servers
  • SharePoint content/search servers
  • Microsoft Graph operation servers
  • Line-of-business custom API servers
  • Internal policy/compliance decision servers

Selection should follow governance, ownership, and API change velocity.

2.7 MCP vs custom connectors: decision matrix

Criteria MCP Custom Connector
Change velocity Better for rapidly evolving tool surfaces Better for stable contracts
Maker discoverability in Power Platform Limited to MCP-enabled contexts Strong in Power Platform UX
DLP and connector governance integration Emerging and architecture-specific Mature policy integration
Reuse across non-Power Platform agent stacks Strong Moderate
Fine-grained low-code control Moderate Strong
Protocol standardization Strong via MCP Strong via OpenAPI/connector model

2.8 MCP configuration best practices

  1. Keep each tool purpose narrowly scoped and explicit.
  2. Define strict schemas with required fields and enums where applicable.
  3. Version tools non-destructively; avoid breaking parameter renames.
  4. Add healthcheck and diagnostic endpoints for fast troubleshooting.
  5. Capture invocation telemetry with tool name, latency, status, and error code.
  6. Document data classification per tool and expected sensitivity levels.
  7. Enforce auth and least privilege at server boundary, not only in client agent.
  8. Implement consistent retry semantics and idempotency behavior for write tools.
  9. Design for graceful degradation when one downstream dependency fails.
  10. Provide human-readable and machine-readable errors together.

2.9 MCP tool definition JSON example

{
  "name": "lookupCustomerByEmail",
  "description": "Finds a customer profile by email for support and order-assistance scenarios. Do not use for anonymous users.",
  "inputSchema": {
    "type": "object",
    "required": ["email"],
    "properties": {
      "email": { "type": "string", "format": "email", "description": "Customer email address" },
      "includeOrders": { "type": "boolean", "default": false }
    }
  },
  "outputSchema": {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {
      "status": { "type": "string", "enum": ["ok", "not_found", "error"] },
      "customer": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "id": { "type": "string" },
          "fullName": { "type": "string" },
          "segment": { "type": "string" }
        }
      },
      "diagnostics": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "correlationId": { "type": "string" },
          "latencyMs": { "type": "integer" }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

3) Power Automate Integration (Agent Flows) Deep Reference

3.1 Copilot Studio to flow invocation model

Copilot Studio topics can call flows through tool/action nodes and YAML definitions. Core integration points:

  • Input variables bound from topic/global/system context
  • Flow execution under user or maker credentials
  • Returned outputs mapped back into topic variables
  • Post-call branching based on status and response content

3.2 InvokeFlowAction YAML syntax example

kind: AdaptiveDialog
beginDialog:
  kind: OnRecognizedIntent
  id: main
  intent:
    displayName: Submit Support Ticket
    triggerQueries:
      - create support ticket
      - open a help request
  actions:
    - kind: Question
      id: askSummary
      variable: Topic.Summary
      prompt: Please summarize the issue.
      entity: StringPrebuiltEntity
    - kind: InvokeFlowAction
      id: callSupportFlow
      flowId: 11111111-2222-3333-4444-555555555555
      input:
        summary: =Topic.Summary
        userEmail: =User.Email
        conversationId: =System.Conversation.Id
      output:
        ticketId: Topic.TicketId
        resultStatus: Topic.FlowStatus
        errorMessage: Topic.FlowError
    - kind: ConditionGroup
      id: checkFlowResult
      conditions:
        - id: success
          condition: =Topic.FlowStatus = "ok"
          actions:
            - kind: SendMessage
              id: msgSuccess
              message: Ticket {Topic.TicketId} has been created.
      elseActions:
        - kind: SendMessage
          id: msgFailure
          message: I could not create the ticket. Details: {Topic.FlowError}

3.3 Designing flow return values (Respond to Power Virtual Agents)

Output contract principles:

  • Always return a machine-readable status field (ok, validation_error, dependency_error, timeout, unknown_error).
  • Return a human-safe summary string for direct user messaging.
  • Include correlation ID for diagnostics.
  • Include optional remediation hints when known.

JSON output pattern:

{
  "status": "ok",
  "ticketId": "INC-482391",
  "message": "Ticket created successfully",
  "correlationId": "9bb2e5ee-8ab7-4d8f-8821-381b9fa57f8f",
  "retryable": false
}

3.4 Structured error handling pattern

Recommended flow architecture:

  1. Initialize normalized response object.
  2. Try scope: business logic and external calls.
  3. Catch scope: map failure to normalized status.
  4. Finally scope: guarantee response emission.
  5. Respond action returns deterministic schema.

Standard error schema:

{
  "status": "dependency_error",
  "error": {
    "code": "CRM_TIMEOUT",
    "message": "CRM did not respond within budget",
    "retryable": true,
    "retryAfterSeconds": 30
  },
  "correlationId": "4f5af67d-e1b1-4280-a9f0-a6f8b81dc023"
}

3.5 Async patterns for agent-integrated flows

Pattern Description Tradeoffs
Fire-and-forget Immediate acknowledge, process continues Fast UX but requires out-of-band status model
Long-running with notification Persist job and notify later (Teams/email/event) More components, strong for heavy workloads
Deferred polling Return job ID, topic polls status endpoint Predictable but increases orchestration complexity
Timeout guard with fallback Abort at time budget and return guidance Prevents hanging interactions

Guardrails:

  • Respect topic interaction latency budget.
  • Persist job state with deterministic status transitions.
  • Include retry policy with exponential backoff for transient dependencies.
  • Ensure idempotency keys for retried submissions.

3.6 Flow template patterns

Template Goal Key Design Controls
Data lookup Read from one source and return formatted summary Use strict input validation and null-safe projection
Form submission Validate payload, write record, return confirmation ID Use idempotency key and duplicate detection
Approval workflow Submit approval, track state, return pending/result Expose status endpoint or follow-up notification
Notification fanout Send message across channels Rate limit and deduplicate recipients
Multi-source aggregation Fan-out to systems and merge response Handle partial failures and provenance tags

3.7 Flow best practices for agent integration

  1. Define explicit timeout budget per dependency and overall flow.
  2. Validate all inputs early and fail fast with actionable error codes.
  3. Use idempotency token for create/update operations to avoid duplicates.
  4. Normalize all downstream errors into a stable output contract.
  5. Return correlation IDs and key diagnostics in all responses.
  6. Avoid large payload responses; return summaries and fetch details on demand.
  7. Use least-privilege connectors and service principals where possible.
  8. Implement retry only for transient classes, not validation errors.
  9. Separate business logic from integration plumbing with scopes and child flows.
  10. Instrument latency and failure metrics for each major action.

3.8 Flow response contract example (Power Fx friendly)

{
  "status": "validation_error",
  "message": "ZIP code must be 5 numeric digits",
  "errors": [
    { "field": "zipCode", "code": "INVALID_FORMAT", "detail": "Expected #####" }
  ],
  "correlationId": "521f8364-d922-4f82-b942-cbd9e88ef847",
  "retryable": false
}

4) Architecture Patterns

4.1 Reference architecture: Connector-first with flow orchestration

User -> Copilot Studio Topic -> InvokeFlowAction -> Power Automate Flow
                                           |
                                           +-> Custom Connector A (CRM)
                                           +-> Custom Connector B (Billing)
                                           +-> Dataverse (state/audit)
Flow -> Normalized Response -> Topic Condition Branch -> User response

4.2 Reference architecture: MCP-first with selective flow fallback

User -> Copilot Studio Generative Orchestrator
   -> MCP Tool Selection -> MCP Server -> API/Data Sources
   -> Optional Flow Invocation for long-running tasks
   -> Consolidated response with citations/status context

4.3 Choosing the integration surface

Requirement Best Fit Reason
Stable API reused by many makers Custom Connector Strong UX, governance, and lifecycle model
Rapidly changing action catalog MCP Server-side evolution with dynamic tool discovery
Complex orchestration with branching and approvals Power Automate Flow Best workflow control and enterprise integrations
Low-latency single lookup Connector action or MCP tool Avoid unnecessary orchestration overhead
Cross-system transaction-like process Flow + connectors Control state, retries, and compensating actions

5) Governance, Security, and Operations

5.1 Identity and authentication controls

  1. Prefer Entra ID OAuth where possible for enterprise APIs.
  2. Separate dev/test/prod app registrations and secrets.
  3. Use managed identities or service principals for non-user automation.
  4. Enforce secret rotation policy and expiry tracking.

5.2 Data protection and DLP alignment

  1. Classify each connector and MCP tool by data sensitivity.
  2. Align DLP connector groupings with business process boundaries.
  3. Block raw HTTP where unmanaged exfiltration risk is unacceptable.
  4. Review cross-tenant API endpoints and legal boundaries.

5.3 Reliability engineering

  1. Instrument p50/p95/p99 latency per operation.
  2. Track failure taxonomies: auth, validation, dependency, timeout.
  3. Define SLOs for critical actions and trigger paths.
  4. Implement synthetic probes for high-value operations.

5.4 ALM and versioning

  1. Version API contract and connector metadata together.
  2. Use semantic versioning and include migration notes for breaking changes.
  3. Gate releases with connector validation and flow regression tests.
  4. Promote via managed solutions and environment variables.

6) Troubleshooting Playbooks

6.x Connector authentication failures

  1. Verify token audience/resource and scope values.
  2. Confirm redirect URI registration and connection re-authentication.
  3. Inspect API gateway logs for invalid_grant/unauthorized_client.
  4. Validate time skew and certificate chain if mutual TLS is involved.

6.x MCP tool not selected by orchestrator

  1. Improve tool description with clear intent keywords.
  2. Check overlapping tools with ambiguous names.
  3. Reduce noisy tools or tighten tool scope descriptions.
  4. Test with varied utterances and analyze selected tool traces.

6.x Flow timeouts in topic invocation

  1. Add timeout guards around slow connector calls.
  2. Move non-critical work to asynchronous branch after immediate response.
  3. Cache lookups where freshness requirements allow.
  4. Return deferred status token instead of blocking user turn.

6.x Binary content upload/download errors

  1. Validate content-type and transfer encoding settings.
  2. Ensure schema models binary as file/byte format appropriately.
  3. Check platform limits and chunking capability configuration.
  4. Use pre-signed URLs if direct transfer is size-constrained.

7) Implementation Checklists

7.1 Custom connector readiness checklist

Check Status Notes
OpenAPI validates without critical errors Pending
Every operation has summary + description Pending
Error schemas are explicit and machine-readable Pending
Auth model tested with least-privilege account Pending
x-ms extensions reviewed for UX and runtime behavior Pending
Pagination and throttling behavior documented Pending
Binary payload operations tested end-to-end Pending
DLP classification approved by admins Pending
CI pipeline runs paconn validate Pending
Release notes and migration guidance published Pending

7.2 MCP readiness checklist

Check Status Notes
Server registration documented with owner and endpoint Pending
Tool descriptions contain intent + exclusions Pending
Schemas include required fields and enums where appropriate Pending
Error contracts are standardized across tools Pending
Observability includes per-tool latency and error metrics Pending
Security review completed for each tool capability Pending
Breaking-change policy defined for tool versions Pending
Fallback behavior defined when server is unavailable Pending
Tool usage analytics reviewed for low-precision tools Pending
Operational runbook exists for incident response Pending

7.3 Flow integration readiness checklist

Check Status Notes
Input validation performed before external calls Pending
Response contract includes status, message, correlationId Pending
Try/Catch scopes normalize dependency errors Pending
Timeout budgets defined per dependency and total flow Pending
Idempotency key supported for mutating operations Pending
Retry policy tuned for transient failures only Pending
Topic-side condition branches cover error statuses Pending
Monitoring dashboard tracks flow success/failure trends Pending
Test suite covers happy path and 4xx/5xx failures Pending
Promotion pipeline updates connection references safely Pending

8) Appendix: Reusable Snippets

8.1 Topic-side error branch pattern (YAML)

- kind: ConditionGroup
  id: routeByStatus
  conditions:
    - id: ok
      condition: =Topic.FlowStatus = "ok"
      actions:
        - kind: SendMessage
          message: Operation succeeded. Reference: {Topic.ReferenceId}
    - id: validation
      condition: =Topic.FlowStatus = "validation_error"
      actions:
        - kind: SendMessage
          message: I need corrected input: {Topic.FlowError}
  elseActions:
    - kind: SendMessage
      message: The service is currently unavailable. Please try again later.

8.2 OpenAPI parameter metadata with dynamic values

parameters:
  - name: region
    in: query
    required: true
    type: string
    x-ms-summary: Region
    x-ms-dynamic-values:
      operationId: ListRegions
      value-path: code
      value-title: name
      parameters: {}

8.3 Normalized flow response object pattern

{
  "status": "ok | validation_error | dependency_error | timeout | unknown_error",
  "message": "Human-readable summary",
  "data": {},
  "errors": [],
  "correlationId": "uuid",
  "retryable": true
}

9) Appendix: Validation and Review Rubric

Area Weight Passing Criteria
API contract quality 20 No critical schema defects; complete operation metadata
Security and auth 20 Least privilege, secret hygiene, auth flows validated
Error handling 15 Deterministic machine-readable errors across surfaces
Performance and resiliency 15 Timeout budgets, retries, idempotency implemented
LLM routing readiness 10 Descriptions and parameter semantics support precise selection
Governance and DLP 10 Classifications and policy fit documented/approved
ALM and observability 10 CI validation, telemetry, and runbooks in place

Recommended release gate: overall score >= 85 and no category below 70.

10) Appendix: Glossary

  • Action: An operation invoked on demand to perform work and return output.
  • Trigger: An operation that starts automation when an event occurs or is detected.
  • DLP: Data Loss Prevention policy mechanism that controls connector combinations.
  • Idempotency: Property where repeated requests have the same effect as one request.
  • MCP: Model Context Protocol for standardized tool/resource exposure to AI systems.
  • OpenAPI: API description standard for HTTP interfaces.
  • Connector: Power Platform abstraction over an API for low-code consumption.
  • Correlation ID: Trace identifier for debugging across distributed components.
  • Retryable Error: Failure likely to succeed on subsequent attempt.
  • Non-retryable Error: Failure requiring input correction or manual intervention.
  • Generative Orchestration: LLM-based selection of topics, tools, and knowledge.
  • InvokeFlowAction: Topic node that calls a Power Automate flow and maps I/O.
  • x-ms-summary: Power Platform extension for user-friendly labels.
  • x-ms-dynamic-values: Extension to load parameter options dynamically from another operation.
  • x-ms-trigger: Extension indicating trigger semantics for an operation.
  • Custom Code: Connector-side script transformation logic for advanced protocol handling.
  • Server-driven Pagination: API returns continuation token or next link for subsequent pages.
  • Webhook Trigger: Push-based trigger where source system calls registered callback URL.
  • Polling Trigger: Pull-based trigger checking source for new or changed data.
  • Managed Solution: Packaged deployment artifact used for ALM promotion in Power Platform.

  139. Implementation note 139: Define explicit ownership, telemetry, and rollback steps for each integration operation to reduce incident mean time to recovery.

  150. Implementation note 150: Define explicit ownership, telemetry, and rollback steps for each integration operation to reduce incident mean time to recovery.

Supplement B: Extended gotcha catalog

  1. Gotcha 1: Validate schema evolution against backward compatibility rules before environment promotion to avoid hidden runtime failures.
  2. Gotcha 2: Validate schema evolution against backward compatibility rules before environment promotion to avoid hidden runtime failures.

dataverse-powerfx.md

tech/agents/power-platform-advisor/knowledge/dataverse-powerfx.md

Dataverse and Power Fx Deep Knowledge Reference

Purpose and Audience

This document is the canonical engineering reference for the Power Platform Advisor agent. It is written for solution architects, makers, professional developers, and governance leads who build production-grade workloads on Microsoft Dataverse and Power Fx in Copilot Studio.

Use this guide when you need implementation-level detail, design trade-offs, and concrete formula patterns.

Scope

This guide covers:

  • Dataverse platform model, schema design, relationships, views, business rules, security, virtual tables, elastic tables, and Web API patterns.
  • Power Fx in Copilot Studio: types, expressions, table shaping, JSON parsing, variable scopes, limits, and reusable patterns.

How to Read This Reference

  • Read Dataverse sections when modeling enterprise data and governance controls.
  • Read Power Fx sections when implementing conversational logic, response shaping, and dynamic runtime behavior in Copilot Studio.
  • Use code snippets directly and adapt table/column logical names to your environment.

Dataverse

Dataverse is the relational data platform for Power Platform, Dynamics 365, and Copilot Studio solution data. It combines metadata-driven schema, role-based security, audited operations, API surface area, and ALM integration.

Dataverse Architectural Fundamentals

  • Metadata-first model: tables, columns, relationships, forms, views, and business rules are all metadata artifacts.
  • Logical names are immutable identifiers and should be treated like API contracts.
  • Display names are mutable and localizable; do not rely on display names in automation.
  • Every row has system columns such as createdon, modifiedon, ownerid, statecode, and statuscode (table dependent).
  • All solution-aware artifacts should be managed through managed/unmanaged solutions and source-controlled via unpacked XML.

Tables (Entities)

Standard vs Custom Tables

Category Description Example Governance Impact
Standard table First-party Microsoft table delivered by Dataverse or Dynamics apps account, contact, incident Upgrades may add columns/forms; avoid unsupported customizations to core behavior
Custom table Tenant-defined table for domain-specific data cr3d_project, cr3d_vendorcontract Full ownership of schema and lifecycle; naming discipline is mandatory

Design guidance:

  • Prefer standard tables when they align with domain semantics and process behavior.
  • Use custom tables when business concept is unique or standard table baggage is unnecessary.
  • Avoid creating near-duplicate custom equivalents for mature standard entities unless isolation is intentional.

Table Types

Table Type Use Case Storage/Execution Model Constraints
Standard Core business transactional data Full Dataverse relational + security + events Best default choice for most line-of-business models
Activity Time-bound interactions (tasks, email, phone calls) Activity pointer model with timeline integration Activity-specific behaviors and relationships
Virtual Data remains in external source Read/write proxied through data provider Limited Dataverse features depending on provider
Elastic High-scale, high-throughput, distributed workloads Optimized for throughput/partitioning Feature parity differs from standard tables; validate requirements

Naming Conventions

Recommended naming template:

  • Publisher prefix: cr3d (example)
  • Table schema name: cr3d_Order
  • Table logical name: cr3d_order
  • Primary name column: cr3d_name
  • Primary key: cr3d_orderid

Naming rules:

  1. Use singular nouns for table names (Project, not Projects).
  2. Keep logical names lowercase snake-like style generated by platform and never rename after deployment.
  3. Prefix every custom artifact with your publisher prefix.
  4. Use stable, API-friendly names (no environment-specific terms like dev_ or temp_).
  5. Treat display names as UI only; all automation should use logical names.

Table Properties

Common table-level properties:

  • Ownership: User or Team-owned vs Organization-owned.
  • Auditing: Enable at table and column level.
  • Change tracking: Required for delta sync patterns.
  • Offline availability: Needed for offline client scenarios.
  • Duplicate detection: Rules for probabilistic match prevention.
  • Notes/activities enablement: Timeline and annotations support.
  • Search indexing inclusion: Dataverse Search scope.
  • Data retention rules: Lifecycle governance and compliance.

Design decision matrix:

Decision Prefer This When
Ownership model User/Team-owned Need row-level ownership security and assignment workflows
Ownership model Organization-owned Data is shared globally with no ownership semantics
Auditing Enabled Regulated processes, approvals, sensitive data changes
Change tracking Enabled Integration consumers need incremental sync

Columns (Fields)

Column Data Types

Data Type Typical Use Notes
Single line of text Names, codes, short labels Supports formatting variants; use max length governance
Multiple lines of text Descriptions, notes Consider rich text and search behavior
Whole number Counts, integer states Use min/max bounds for data quality
Decimal number Precise fractional values Use precision per domain requirement
Currency Monetary values Includes exchange rate handling in multi-currency orgs
Date and time Events, due dates, transactions Choose behavior (user local, date only, timezone independent) carefully
Lookup Referential link to another row Enforces relationship integrity and navigation
Choice Controlled vocabulary Use global choice where reuse needed
Yes/No Binary flags Suitable for explicit booleans only
File Binary documents Prefer when file is part of record lifecycle
Image Record-associated image Great for profile/product visuals

Column Properties

Important properties to configure explicitly:

  • Required level: Business required, recommended, optional.
  • Searchable: Whether included in quick find / Dataverse search index config.
  • Auditing: Track who changed what and when.
  • Security: Field-level security eligible on supported columns.
  • Validations: Min/max values, max length, format constraints.
  • Default values: Carefully chosen defaults reduce maker logic complexity.
  • Behavior for date/time columns: strongest source of downstream defects if misconfigured.

Calculated Columns

Calculated columns are server-side expressions evaluated on record operations based on other columns.

Use cases:

  • SLA target date from created date + priority.
  • Derived status labels.
  • Numeric computations shared across forms and APIs.

Implementation notes:

  • Evaluate server-side consistently across apps.
  • Avoid overloading calculated logic with very complex branching when plugin or flow is clearer.

Rollup Columns

Rollup columns aggregate related table data, often on schedule.

Use cases:

  • Total invoice amount per account.
  • Count of open cases per customer.
  • Last activity date from related activity table.

Caveats:

  • Aggregation timing is not always immediate.
  • Understand recalculation schedules and on-demand recalc behavior.

Formula Columns

Formula columns provide expression-based computed values with modern formula authoring.

Use cases:

  • Concatenated business labels.
  • Conditional categorization logic.
  • Lightweight computed values where real-time server evaluation is needed.

Relationships

Relationship Types

Type Dataverse Semantics Example
1:N One parent has many children Account has many Contacts
N:1 Child points to one parent (reverse of 1:N) Contact belongs to one Account
N:N Many rows on both sides, via intersect relation User can belong to many Projects and Project has many Users

Cascading Behavior

Cascading controls behavior when parent records are assigned, shared, reparented, or deleted.

Operation Typical Cascade Options Design Warning
Assign Cascade all, active, none Unintended ownership reassignment can break security expectations
Share/Unshare Cascade options Over-sharing risk in sensitive domains
Delete Remove link, restrict, cascade delete Cascade delete can cause destructive data loss if misconfigured
Reparent Cascade options Data visibility changes can be broad

Relationship Roles

Relationship roles provide semantic role labeling for related connections.

Examples:

  • Contact role on account: Decision maker, Finance approver.
  • Partner role on opportunity: Reseller, Distributor.

Use roles to improve clarity in relationship-driven business processes and reporting.

Views

Views define query + projection metadata for model-driven experiences and can influence discovery/search patterns.

View Types

View Type Owner Purpose
System view System customization Organization-wide canonical filtered lists
Personal view Individual user User-specific working sets
Public view Shared customization context Team-shared lists, often curated by admins
Quick find view Table-level search scope Determines searchable columns in quick find
Associated view Subgrid context Controls related records shown from parent forms

View Design

Best practices:

  1. Keep columns task-oriented; avoid overloaded wide views.
  2. Index/filter on columns frequently used in where clauses.
  3. Use clear display names and predictable sort order.
  4. Keep quick find searchable columns bounded to high-value attributes.

Example filter strategy table:

Scenario Filter Pattern
Active records queue statecode = Active and ownerid = Current User
SLA breach watch duedate < Today and status != Closed
Data quality cleanup requiredColumn Is Blank

Business Rules

Business rules provide no-code conditional UI/data logic across model-driven forms and server evaluations (scope dependent).

Conditions

Condition operators commonly include equals, not equals, contains data, does not contain data, greater than, less than.

Actions

Action Behavior Example
Set field value Programmatically assign value Set priority to High when amount > 100000
Set visibility Show/hide control Hide discount section for non-manager role indicators
Set business required Toggle requiredness Require approval reason when status = Rejected
Show error message Block invalid state Show error when end date earlier than start date

Scope

Scope Option Applies To Guidance
Entity Server and all forms where supported Use for cross-client consistency
All forms All model-driven forms for table Use when behavior should be UI-wide
Specific form One form Use for specialized process forms only

Choices (Option Sets)

Local vs Global Choices

Choice Type Reusability Governance
Local choice Bound to one table column Fast to create, can lead to duplication
Global choice Reused across columns/tables Preferred for enterprise vocabularies

Recommendations:

  • Use global choices for statuses, risk ratings, categories reused in multiple domains.
  • Reserve local choices for one-off table semantics.

Multi-Select Choices

Use multi-select when a record can hold multiple categorical tags.

Caveats:

  • Query complexity increases.
  • Reporting and integration mappings need explicit handling.

Dependent Choices

Dependent choices constrain valid child options based on parent selection (for example Country -> State).

Governance note:

  • Validate cross-system integrations respect dependency logic to prevent invalid combinations.

Polymorphic Lookups

Polymorphic lookup columns can target more than one table type.

Lookup Targets Typical Scenario
Customer Account or Contact Case linked to either company or individual
Regarding Multiple activity-enabled tables Activity references varied business records
Owner User or Team Ownership/assignment model

Design considerations:

  • API consumers must inspect lookup logical name annotations to determine concrete type.
  • UI logic should handle type-specific rendering and commands.

Virtual Tables

Virtual tables expose external data in Dataverse without physically copying data.

Integration Models

  • OData v4 providers for RESTful external datasets.
  • Custom data providers for proprietary/legacy systems.

When to Use

Use virtual tables when:

  • Data residency requires source-of-truth to remain external.
  • You need near-real-time access with minimal duplication.
  • Basic CRUD and browsing are sufficient for scenario capabilities.

Avoid or validate deeply when:

  • Advanced Dataverse features are required (auditing depth, offline, complex transactions, some business logic layers).
  • Latency to external source is unpredictable.

Elastic Tables

Elastic tables are built for very high throughput and large-scale event-like or telemetry-like scenarios.

Characteristics

  • Optimized for scale and write throughput.
  • Partition-oriented architecture.
  • Intended for patterns where standard relational transaction semantics may not be primary requirement.

Standard vs Elastic Decision Guide

Requirement Standard Table Elastic Table
Rich relational behavior Strong fit Limited/validate
Highest transactional throughput Moderate Strong fit
Complex model-driven customization Strong fit Scenario-dependent
Massive event ingestion Can bottleneck Strong fit

Security

Dataverse security is layered and composable.

Security Roles

Security roles define privileges (Create, Read, Write, Delete, Append, Append To, Assign, Share) with depth levels.

Depth levels commonly include:

  • User (basic)
  • Business Unit
  • Parent:Child Business Units
  • Organization

Business Units

Business units partition organizational hierarchy and affect role privilege scope.

Design guidance:

  • Keep hierarchy aligned to security boundary needs, not org chart volatility.
  • Avoid deep hierarchies without clear access rationale.

Teams

Team patterns:

  • Owner teams for shared record ownership.
  • Access teams for ad-hoc collaboration without ownership transfer.

Field-Level Security Profiles

Use field-level security for sensitive columns such as salary, personal identifiers, or regulated values.

Key point:

  • Field security protects at column granularity even when table read access exists.

Row-Level Security (RLS)

Dataverse row access is enforced by ownership + role privilege depth + sharing/team mechanics.

RLS implementation tools:

  • Ownership model.
  • Role depth.
  • Sharing.
  • Team ownership.
  • Hierarchy security overlays.

Hierarchy Security

Hierarchy security can grant managers or positional hierarchies visibility into subordinate records.

Use with care:

  • Validate for overexposure in matrix organizations.
  • Document exception patterns and legal constraints.

Dataverse Web API

The Dataverse Web API is OData-compliant and supports CRUD, association management, batch, metadata operations, and change tracking.

CRUD Operations

Create example:

POST https://{org}.crm.dynamics.com/api/data/v9.2/accounts
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "name": "Contoso Manufacturing",
  "telephone1": "+1-555-0100"
}

Retrieve example:

GET https://{org}.crm.dynamics.com/api/data/v9.2/accounts({accountid})?$select=name,telephone1,createdon

Update example:

PATCH https://{org}.crm.dynamics.com/api/data/v9.2/accounts({accountid})
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "telephone1": "+1-555-9999"
}

Delete example:

DELETE https://{org}.crm.dynamics.com/api/data/v9.2/accounts({accountid})

OData Query Patterns

GET /api/data/v9.2/contacts?$select=fullname,emailaddress1&$filter=statecode eq 0&$orderby=createdon desc&$top=50

GET /api/data/v9.2/accounts?$select=name&$expand=primarycontactid($select=fullname,emailaddress1)

GET /api/data/v9.2/incidents?$select=title,ticketnumber&$filter=contains(title,'urgent')

Batch Requests

Batch allows multiple operations in one HTTP call for throughput and reduced network overhead.

POST /api/data/v9.2/$batch
Content-Type: multipart/mixed;boundary=batch_123

--batch_123
Content-Type: application/http
Content-Transfer-Encoding:binary

GET /api/data/v9.2/accounts?$top=10 HTTP/1.1

--batch_123
Content-Type: application/http
Content-Transfer-Encoding:binary

POST /api/data/v9.2/tasks HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/json

{"subject":"Follow up"}
--batch_123--

Change Tracking

Change tracking enables incremental extraction and synchronization patterns.

Pattern:

  1. Enable change tracking on table.
  2. Query with delta links where supported.
  3. Persist token externally.
  4. Requery for deltas only.

Dataverse Web API Performance and Reliability Practices

  • Use $select aggressively; never pull full rows if only a few columns are needed.
  • Use server-side filters before pagination.
  • Handle 429 throttling with backoff and retry-after semantics.
  • Keep association/disassociation operations explicit and idempotent where possible.
  • For integrations, map logical names and option values centrally.

Power Fx in Copilot Studio

Power Fx is the low-code formula language used in Copilot Studio expressions, conditions, variable assignments, and response shaping.

Formula Language Fundamentals

  • Strongly typed: values carry explicit or inferred types.
  • Functional: formulas return values from expressions rather than imperative statements.
  • Excel-inspired: function naming and expression composition feel familiar to spreadsheet users.
  • Case-sensitive in many practical authoring contexts for identifiers and member access; keep naming exact to avoid runtime errors.
  • Immutable expression style: use With() and table transformations to avoid opaque side effects.

Copilot Studio-specific principles:

  1. Keep formulas short and composable due to authoring constraints.
  2. Move reusable logic into variables and scoped records.
  3. Validate API response shape before dereferencing nested properties.

Data Types

Type Description Example
Text String content "Hello"
Number Numeric value 42, 3.14
Boolean True/false true
DateTime Date/time value Now()
Table Sequence of records Table({Name:"A"},{Name:"B"})
Record Named fields {Name:"A", Score:90}
Choice Enumerated option value Topic.Priority from choice-backed source
Blank Null-like absence Blank()
GUID Identifier GUID("8f0f8f2b-9f1f-4f55-bec6-2cbe2af7f9aa")

Type handling guidance:

  • Use Text(), Value(), Boolean() conversions when parsing untyped JSON.
  • Use Coalesce() to provide defaults for blanks.
  • Prefer explicit casting in branch outputs to keep type consistency.

Variable Scopes in Copilot Studio

Scope Prefix Lifetime Typical Use
Topic Topic.VarName Current topic/dialog execution Intermediate calculation and branch state
Global Global.VarName Across topics in session Shared user context or flow output reuse
System System.* Runtime-provided User profile, locale, channel metadata
Environment Environment.* Environment configuration App-level settings, endpoints, feature flags

Examples:

Topic.GreetingName = Coalesce(System.User.DisplayName, "there")

Global.IsPremiumUser = Topic.AccountTier = "Premium"

Topic.ApiBase = Environment.CrmApiBaseUrl

Comparison Operators

Operator Meaning Example
= Equal Topic.Count = 0
<> Not equal Topic.Status <> "Closed"
< Less than Topic.Amount < 1000
<= Less than or equal Topic.Score <= 70
> Greater than Topic.RiskScore > 80
>= Greater than or equal Topic.DaysOpen >= 30
in Case-insensitive containment/membership "vip" in Lower(Topic.Tags)
exactin Case-sensitive containment/membership "VIP" exactin Topic.Tags

Text Functions

Concatenate

Concatenate("Hello ", Coalesce(System.User.DisplayName, "there"), ". I can help with your Dataverse request.")

Len

If(Len(Topic.CustomerInput) > 200, "Input too long", "Input accepted")

Left, Right, Mid

Left(Topic.TicketNumber, 3)

Right(Topic.TicketNumber, 4)

Mid(Topic.TicketNumber, 4, 5)

Upper, Lower

Upper(Topic.CustomerCountryCode)

Lower(System.User.Email)

Trim

Trim(Topic.CustomerInput)

Substitute

Substitute(Topic.RawPhone, "-", "")

Split

Split(Topic.CsvTags, ",")

Find

If(Find("urgent", Lower(Topic.Subject)) > 0, true, false)

StartsWith and EndsWith

StartsWith(Lower(Topic.Email), "admin@")

EndsWith(Lower(Topic.FileName), ".pdf")

String Interpolation

$"Hello {Coalesce(System.User.DisplayName, "there")}, your case {Topic.CaseNumber} is {Topic.CaseStatus}."

Logical Functions

If

If(Topic.HasOpenCase, "I found an open case.", "No open cases found.")

Switch

Switch(
    Topic.Intent,
    "CheckOrder", "Let me check your order status.",
    "CancelOrder", "I can help cancel your order.",
    "CreateCase", "I will create a support case.",
    "I did not recognize that request."
)

And, Or, Not

And(Topic.IsAuthenticated, Topic.HasPermission)

Or(Topic.IsVip, Topic.IsEscalated)

Not(Topic.IsClosed)

IsBlank

If(IsBlank(Topic.AccountId), "Please provide an account ID.", "Thanks, processing now.")

IsError

If(IsError(Topic.ApiResult), "I encountered an API error.", "API call succeeded.")

IfError

IfError(
    Value(Topic.AmountText),
    0
)

Coalesce

Coalesce(Topic.Nickname, System.User.DisplayName, "customer")

Number Functions

Abs

Abs(Topic.BalanceDelta)

Round, RoundUp, RoundDown

Round(Topic.Probability, 2)

RoundUp(Topic.EstimateHours, 0)

RoundDown(Topic.DiscountRate * 100, 0)

Int

Int(Topic.SessionDurationMinutes)

Mod

Mod(Topic.SequenceNumber, 2)

Max, Min

Max(Topic.ScoreA, Topic.ScoreB, Topic.ScoreC)

Min(Topic.ScoreA, Topic.ScoreB, Topic.ScoreC)

Sum, Average, CountRows

Sum(Topic.LineItems, Amount)

Average(Topic.LineItems, Amount)

CountRows(Topic.LineItems)

Rand

If(Rand() < 0.5, "VariantA", "VariantB")

Date and Time Functions

Now and Today

Now()

Today()

Year, Month, Day, Hour

Year(Now())

Month(Now())

Day(Now())

Hour(Now())

DateAdd

DateAdd(Today(), 7, TimeUnit.Days)

DateDiff

DateDiff(Topic.CreatedOn, Now(), TimeUnit.Hours)

DateValue

DateValue(Topic.InputDateText)

Text formatting

Text(Now(), "[$-en-US]yyyy-mm-dd hh:mm")

Table Functions

Filter

Filter(Topic.Cases, Status = "Open" && Priority = "High")

Sort

Sort(Topic.Cases, CreatedOn, SortOrder.Descending)

First and Last

First(Topic.Cases)

Last(Topic.Cases)

LookUp

LookUp(Topic.Cases, TicketNumber = Topic.RequestedTicket)

Search

Search(Topic.Cases, Topic.SearchText, "Title", "Description")

AddColumns, DropColumns, RenameColumns, ShowColumns

AddColumns(Topic.Cases, "IsOverdue", DateDiff(DueDate, Now(), TimeUnit.Hours) < 0)

DropColumns(Topic.Cases, "InternalNotes")

RenameColumns(Topic.Cases, "Title", "CaseTitle")

ShowColumns(Topic.Cases, "TicketNumber", "Title", "Status")

ForAll

ForAll(Topic.Cases, { Ticket: TicketNumber, IsOpen: Status = "Open" })

CountRows

CountRows(Filter(Topic.Cases, Status = "Open"))

Collect

Collect(Topic.CaseSummaries, ShowColumns(Topic.Cases, "TicketNumber", "Status"))

JSON Functions

ParseJSON

Topic.JsonObj = ParseJSON(Topic.ApiResponseText)

JSON serialization

JSON({ TicketNumber: Topic.CaseNumber, Status: Topic.CaseStatus })

Untyped extraction patterns

Text(Topic.JsonObj.customer.name)

Value(Topic.JsonObj.order.total)

Boolean(Topic.JsonObj.flags.isEscalated)

ForAll(Table(Topic.JsonObj.items), { Sku: Text(ThisRecord.Value.sku), Qty: Value(ThisRecord.Value.quantity) })

Nested JSON navigation example

With(
    { root: ParseJSON(Topic.ApiResponseText) },
    $"Order {Text(root.order.number)} for {Text(root.customer.name)} totals {Text(Value(root.order.total), "[$-en-US]$#,##0.00")}"
)

Record and Scope

With function

With(
    {
        normalizedEmail: Lower(Trim(Topic.EmailInput)),
        normalizedTicket: Upper(Trim(Topic.TicketInput))
    },
    If(
        EndsWith(normalizedEmail, "@contoso.com"),
        $"Ticket {normalizedTicket} belongs to internal user {normalizedEmail}.",
        $"Ticket {normalizedTicket} belongs to external user {normalizedEmail}."
    )
)

Record creation syntax

{
  Greeting: "Hello",
  UserName: Coalesce(System.User.DisplayName, "there"),
  Timestamp: Now()
}

Field access

Topic.CurrentCase.TicketNumber

First(Topic.Cases).Status

Error Handling

IsBlankOrError

If(IsBlankOrError(Topic.ApiResponseText), "No API response available.", "Response received.")

IfError with fallback

IfError(
    Text(DateValue(Topic.RequestedDateText), "[$-en-US]yyyy-mm-dd"),
    "Invalid date format"
)

Error propagation strategy

Pattern:

  1. Parse source value with IfError.
  2. Store explicit error text in topic variable.
  3. Branch conversation flow on whether error variable is blank.
Topic.ParsedAmount = IfError(Value(Topic.AmountText), Blank())

Topic.AmountError = If(IsBlank(Topic.ParsedAmount), "Amount is invalid", Blank())

Delegation and Limits in Copilot Studio

Copilot Studio formula evaluation is typically in-memory for topic context data and action outputs.

Important constraints:

  • Delegation warnings commonly seen in canvas apps are not surfaced the same way in Copilot Studio authoring.
  • Formula length limit in many Copilot Studio expression surfaces is approximately 1000 characters.
  • Break long logic into staged variables (Topic.Step1, Topic.Step2) to remain maintainable.

Guidance:

  1. Keep each formula single-purpose.
  2. Normalize input once and reuse.
  3. Convert untyped JSON immediately after parse.
  4. Avoid deeply nested If expressions; prefer Switch or With decomposition.

Common Copilot Studio Patterns

Pattern 1: Greeting with user name

$"Hello {Coalesce(System.User.DisplayName, "there")}. I can help with Dataverse records, status checks, and updates."

Pattern 2: Conditional response by intent confidence

If(
    Topic.IntentConfidence >= 0.8,
    $"I am confident you want {Topic.IntentName}.",
    "I am not fully confident yet. Could you rephrase your request?"
)

Pattern 3: Table formatting for adaptive card input

ForAll(
    ShowColumns(FirstN(Topic.Cases, 5), "TicketNumber", "Title", "Status"),
    {
        type: "TextBlock",
        text: $"{TicketNumber} | {Title} | {Status}"
    }
)

Pattern 4: API result parsing with fallback

With(
    { root: ParseJSON(Topic.ApiResponseText) },
    If(
        IsBlankOrError(root),
        "I could not parse the service response.",
        $"Order {Text(root.order.id)} is {Text(root.order.status)}."
    )
)

Pattern 5: Validate required inputs before action

If(
    Or(IsBlank(Topic.AccountId), IsBlank(Topic.RequestType)),
    "Please provide both account ID and request type.",
    "Thanks. I will proceed."
)

Pattern 6: Safe numeric parse from user input

With(
    { parsedQty: IfError(Value(Topic.QuantityText), Blank()) },
    If(
        IsBlank(parsedQty),
        "Quantity must be a number.",
        If(parsedQty <= 0, "Quantity must be greater than zero.", $"Quantity accepted: {Text(parsedQty)}")
    )
)

Pattern 7: Date normalization for API payload

With(
    { parsedDate: IfError(DateValue(Topic.DateInput), Blank()) },
    If(
        IsBlank(parsedDate),
        "Date is invalid. Use YYYY-MM-DD.",
        Text(parsedDate, "[$-en-US]yyyy-mm-dd")
    )
)

Pattern 8: Choice label mapping

Switch(
    Topic.PriorityCode,
    1, "Low",
    2, "Normal",
    3, "High",
    4, "Critical",
    "Unknown"
)

Power Fx Function Reference Tables

Text Function Quick Reference

Function Signature Pattern Copilot Studio Example
Concatenate Concatenate(text1, text2, ...) Concatenate("Case ", Topic.CaseNumber)
Len Len(text) Len(Topic.UserInput)
Left Left(text, count) Left(Topic.CaseNumber, 3)
Right Right(text, count) Right(Topic.CaseNumber, 4)
Mid Mid(text, start, count) Mid(Topic.CaseNumber, 4, 4)
Upper Upper(text) Upper(Topic.Region)
Lower Lower(text) Lower(System.User.Email)
Trim Trim(text) Trim(Topic.UserInput)
Substitute Substitute(text, old, new) Substitute(Topic.Phone, " ", "")
Split Split(text, separator) Split(Topic.TagsCsv, ",")
Find Find(findText, withinText) Find("error", Lower(Topic.Log))
StartsWith StartsWith(text, startText) StartsWith(Topic.Command, "/")
EndsWith EndsWith(text, endText) EndsWith(Lower(Topic.FileName), ".json")

Logical Function Quick Reference

Function Signature Pattern Copilot Studio Example
If If(condition, then, else) If(Topic.IsKnownUser, "Welcome back", "Welcome")
Switch Switch(expr, match1, result1, ..., default) Switch(Topic.Intent, "Track", "Tracking", "Help")
And And(cond1, cond2, ...) And(Topic.Authenticated, Topic.ActiveSession)
Or Or(cond1, cond2, ...) Or(Topic.IsAdmin, Topic.IsSupervisor)
Not Not(condition) Not(Topic.IsClosed)
IsBlank IsBlank(value) IsBlank(Topic.OrderId)
IsError IsError(value) IsError(Topic.ParseResult)
IfError IfError(value, fallback) IfError(Value(Topic.Cost), 0)
Coalesce Coalesce(v1, v2, ...) Coalesce(Topic.Name, "Guest")

Number Function Quick Reference

Function Signature Pattern Copilot Studio Example
Abs Abs(number) Abs(Topic.Delta)
Round Round(number, digits) Round(Topic.Score, 1)
RoundUp RoundUp(number, digits) RoundUp(Topic.Hours, 0)
RoundDown RoundDown(number, digits) RoundDown(Topic.Rate, 2)
Int Int(number) Int(Topic.Minutes / 60)
Mod Mod(number, divisor) Mod(Topic.Counter, 10)
Max Max(n1, n2, ...) Max(Topic.A, Topic.B)
Min Min(n1, n2, ...) Min(Topic.A, Topic.B)
Sum Sum(table, formula) Sum(Topic.Items, Amount)
Average Average(table, formula) Average(Topic.Items, Score)
CountRows CountRows(table) CountRows(Topic.Items)
Rand Rand() If(Rand() < .5, "A", "B")

Date/Time Function Quick Reference

Function Signature Pattern Copilot Studio Example
Now Now() Now()
Today Today() Today()
Year Year(datetime) Year(Now())
Month Month(datetime) Month(Now())
Day Day(datetime) Day(Now())
Hour Hour(datetime) Hour(Now())
DateAdd DateAdd(date, amount, unit) DateAdd(Today(), 30, TimeUnit.Days)
DateDiff DateDiff(start, end, unit) DateDiff(Topic.OpenedOn, Now(), TimeUnit.Days)
DateValue DateValue(text) DateValue(Topic.InputDate)
Text Text(value, format) Text(Now(), "[$-en-US]yyyy-mm-dd")

Table Function Quick Reference

Function Signature Pattern Copilot Studio Example
Filter Filter(table, condition) Filter(Topic.Cases, Status="Open")
Sort Sort(table, formula, order) Sort(Topic.Cases, CreatedOn, SortOrder.Descending)
First First(table) First(Topic.Cases)
Last Last(table) Last(Topic.Cases)
LookUp LookUp(table, condition) LookUp(Topic.Cases, TicketNumber=Topic.Ticket)
Search Search(table, text, col1,...) Search(Topic.Cases, Topic.Term, "Title")
AddColumns AddColumns(table, name, formula...) AddColumns(Topic.Cases, "AgeDays", DateDiff(CreatedOn,Now(),TimeUnit.Days))
DropColumns DropColumns(table, col...) DropColumns(Topic.Cases, "RawJson")
RenameColumns RenameColumns(table, old, new...) RenameColumns(Topic.Cases, "Title", "CaseTitle")
ShowColumns ShowColumns(table, col...) ShowColumns(Topic.Cases, "TicketNumber", "Status")
ForAll ForAll(table, formula) ForAll(Topic.Cases, TicketNumber)
CountRows CountRows(table) CountRows(Topic.Cases)
Collect Collect(collection, item/table) Collect(Topic.Cache, Topic.Cases)

Copilot Studio API Response Parsing Recipes

Recipe: Dataverse-like OData response

Sample response:

{
  "value": [
    { "ticketnumber": "CAS-1001", "title": "Login issue", "statecode": 0 },
    { "ticketnumber": "CAS-1002", "title": "Payment failed", "statecode": 1 }
  ]
}

Formula:

With(
    { root: ParseJSON(Topic.ApiResponseText) },
    ForAll(
        Table(root.value),
        {
            Ticket: Text(ThisRecord.Value.ticketnumber),
            Title: Text(ThisRecord.Value.title),
            StateCode: Value(ThisRecord.Value.statecode)
        }
    )
)

Recipe: Single object payload

{
  "order": {
    "id": "ORD-7782",
    "status": "Shipped",
    "eta": "2026-02-11"
  }
}

With(
    { root: ParseJSON(Topic.ApiResponseText) },
    {
        OrderId: Text(root.order.id),
        Status: Text(root.order.status),
        EtaDate: DateValue(Text(root.order.eta))
    }
)

Recipe: Error envelope

{
  "error": {
    "code": "BadRequest",
    "message": "Invalid account ID"
  }
}

With(
    { root: ParseJSON(Topic.ApiResponseText) },
    If(
        Not(IsBlankOrError(root.error)),
        $"Service error: {Text(root.error.code)} - {Text(root.error.message)}",
        "No service error"
    )
)

Dataverse and Power Fx Combined Patterns

Pattern A: Build OData filter text from user intent

With(
    {
        safeStatus: Coalesce(Topic.RequestedStatus, "Open"),
        safeTop: IfError(Int(Topic.TopN), 10)
    },
    $"$select=ticketnumber,title,statuscode&$filter=statuscode eq '{safeStatus}'&$top={Text(safeTop)}"
)

Pattern B: Dynamic status summary message

With(
    { openCount: CountRows(Filter(Topic.Cases, Status = "Open")) },
    If(
        openCount = 0,
        "You have no open cases.",
        $"You have {Text(openCount)} open cases."
    )
)

Pattern C: Normalize and validate account identifier

With(
    {
        cleanId: Upper(Substitute(Trim(Topic.AccountIdInput), "-", ""))
    },
    If(
        Len(cleanId) <> 12,
        "Account ID must be 12 characters after normalization.",
        cleanId
    )
)

Governance Notes for Agent Authors

  • Never embed credentials in formulas or prompt text.
  • Keep service endpoint references in environment variables.
  • Limit personally identifiable information in transient variables.
  • Log only minimally necessary diagnostic data.
  • Respect Dataverse security boundaries; do not imply access beyond role permissions.

Troubleshooting Guide

Symptom: Formula returns blank unexpectedly

Checks:

  1. Verify source variable is initialized in topic path.
  2. Confirm casing for property names from parsed JSON.
  3. Use Coalesce to guard blank values.

Symptom: ParseJSON fields not accessible

Checks:

  1. Validate raw JSON shape in test transcript.
  2. Wrap arrays with Table(...) before ForAll.
  3. Convert untyped fields using Text/Value/Boolean.

Symptom: Formula too long

Mitigations:

  • Split logic into chained Topic.* assignments.
  • Use With for local readability and reuse.
  • Replace repeated expressions with intermediate variables.

Symptom: Incorrect date interpretation

Mitigations:

  • Normalize to ISO date strings before DateValue.
  • Use explicit Text formatting with locale token.
  • Clarify timezone assumptions in user prompts.

Production Readiness Checklist

Dataverse Checklist

  • Table ownership model chosen intentionally.
  • Security roles validated with least privilege principle.
  • Relationship cascading reviewed and documented.
  • Choice taxonomy aligned to enterprise vocabulary.
  • Auditing enabled where compliance requires.
  • Web API integrations use $select and bounded queries.

Power Fx Checklist

  • All user input normalized and validated.
  • ParseJSON outputs cast to typed values.
  • Error branches implemented with user-safe fallback messages.
  • Formula length and readability constraints respected.
  • Variable scopes (Topic, Global, System, Environment) used appropriately.

Appendix A: Extended Power Fx Examples by Scenario

Scenario 1: Case triage response

With(
    {
        highOpen: CountRows(Filter(Topic.Cases, Status = "Open" && Priority = "High")),
        totalOpen: CountRows(Filter(Topic.Cases, Status = "Open"))
    },
    If(
        totalOpen = 0,
        "There are no open cases.",
        If(
            highOpen > 0,
            $"There are {Text(totalOpen)} open cases, including {Text(highOpen)} high-priority cases.",
            $"There are {Text(totalOpen)} open cases and none are high priority."
        )
    )
)

Scenario 2: Guided data confirmation

With(
    {
        nameValue: Coalesce(Trim(Topic.CustomerName), ""),
        emailValue: Lower(Trim(Topic.CustomerEmail))
    },
    If(
        Or(IsBlank(nameValue), IsBlank(emailValue)),
        "Please provide both your name and email.",
        If(
            Not(EndsWith(emailValue, "@contoso.com")),
            "Please provide a corporate email ending with @contoso.com.",
            $"Thanks {nameValue}. I confirmed your email as {emailValue}."
        )
    )
)

Scenario 3: Parse and summarize line items

With(
    { root: ParseJSON(Topic.OrderJsonText) },
    With(
        {
            typedItems: ForAll(
                Table(root.items),
                {
                    Sku: Text(ThisRecord.Value.sku),
                    Qty: Value(ThisRecord.Value.qty),
                    Price: Value(ThisRecord.Value.price)
                }
            )
        },
        $"Order has {Text(CountRows(typedItems))} lines and total {Text(Sum(typedItems, Qty * Price), "[$-en-US]$#,##0.00")}."
    )
)

Appendix B: Dataverse Design Anti-Patterns

Anti-Pattern Why It Fails in Production Better Alternative
Random naming across solutions Breaks discoverability and API consistency Enforce publisher prefix and schema convention policy
Overuse of local choices Inconsistent semantics and reporting pain Promote reusable vocab to global choices
Unreviewed cascade delete Accidental destructive deletes Use restrict/remove link and explicit archive process
Security role sprawl Hard to audit, risky privilege drift Role templates + periodic access reviews
Fat formulas with deep nesting Hard to debug within length constraints Compose with With and staged topic variables
Parsing JSON without casting Type errors and blank propagation Immediately cast with Text, Value, Boolean

Appendix C: Quick Authoring Snippets

Snippet: Safe display name

Coalesce(System.User.DisplayName, "there")

Snippet: Safe numeric fallback

IfError(Value(Topic.AmountText), 0)

Snippet: Build concise response line

$"Case {Topic.CaseNumber}: {Topic.CaseStatus}"

Snippet: Minimal JSON extraction

With({j: ParseJSON(Topic.ApiResponseText)}, Text(j.id))

Snippet: Blank guard

If(IsBlank(Topic.RequiredInput), "Missing input", "OK")

Closing Notes

This reference is intended to be versioned with the Power Platform Advisor agent. Update it whenever Dataverse capabilities, Copilot Studio formula behaviors, or organizational standards evolve.

Recommended maintenance cycle:

  • Review monthly for platform changes.
  • Review after major ALM or governance policy updates.
  • Add tested snippets from production incidents and postmortems.

Appendix D: Function-by-Function Copilot Studio Examples

The following catalog provides concrete one-line and multi-line formulas for practical reuse.

Text Examples

Function Example
Concatenate Concatenate("User: ", Coalesce(System.User.DisplayName, "unknown"))
Len Len(Trim(Topic.UserQuestion))
Left Left(Topic.ReferenceCode, 5)
Right Right(Topic.ReferenceCode, 3)
Mid Mid(Topic.ReferenceCode, 3, 4)
Upper Upper(Topic.RegionCode)
Lower Lower(Topic.EmailInput)
Trim Trim(Topic.FreeText)
Substitute Substitute(Topic.FreeText, Char(10), " ")
Split Split(Topic.Tags, ";")
Find Find("refund", Lower(Topic.IntentText))
StartsWith StartsWith(Lower(Topic.IntentText), "create")
EndsWith EndsWith(Lower(Topic.AttachmentName), ".csv")

Logical Examples

Function Example
If If(Topic.IsKnown, "Known user", "New user")
Switch Switch(Topic.Channel, "teams", "Teams", "web", "Web", "Other")
And And(Topic.IsAuthenticated, Not(IsBlank(Topic.AccountId)))
Or Or(Topic.IsRetry, Topic.IsEscalationRequested)
Not Not(Topic.UserConfirmed)
IsBlank IsBlank(Topic.OrderId)
IsError IsError(Topic.PayloadJson)
IfError IfError(DateValue(Topic.InputDate), Blank())
Coalesce Coalesce(Topic.Nickname, Topic.FullName, "Customer")

Logical Example 1: If

If(Topic.IsKnown, "Known user", "New user")

Copilot Studio note: This If pattern is suitable for topic-level logic and response shaping.

Logical Example 2: Switch

Switch(Topic.Channel, "teams", "Teams", "web", "Web", "Other")

Copilot Studio note: This Switch pattern is suitable for topic-level logic and response shaping.

Logical Example 3: And

And(Topic.IsAuthenticated, Not(IsBlank(Topic.AccountId)))

Copilot Studio note: This And pattern is suitable for topic-level logic and response shaping.

Logical Example 4: Or

Or(Topic.IsRetry, Topic.IsEscalationRequested)

Copilot Studio note: This Or pattern is suitable for topic-level logic and response shaping.

Logical Example 5: Not

Not(Topic.UserConfirmed)

Copilot Studio note: This Not pattern is suitable for topic-level logic and response shaping.

Logical Example 6: IsBlank

IsBlank(Topic.OrderId)

Copilot Studio note: This IsBlank pattern is suitable for topic-level logic and response shaping.

Logical Example 7: IsError

IsError(Topic.PayloadJson)

Copilot Studio note: This IsError pattern is suitable for topic-level logic and response shaping.

Logical Example 8: IfError

IfError(DateValue(Topic.InputDate), Blank())

Copilot Studio note: This IfError pattern is suitable for topic-level logic and response shaping.

Logical Example 9: Coalesce

Coalesce(Topic.Nickname, Topic.FullName, "Customer")

Copilot Studio note: This Coalesce pattern is suitable for topic-level logic and response shaping.

Function Example

RoundUp RoundUp(Topic.EffortHours, 0)

Int Int(Topic.ElapsedMinutes / 60)
Mod Mod(Topic.AttemptCount, 3)
Max Max(Topic.MetricA, Topic.MetricB, Topic.MetricC)
Min Min(Topic.MetricA, Topic.MetricB, Topic.MetricC)
Sum Sum(Topic.Rows, Amount)
Average Average(Topic.Rows, Amount)
CountRows CountRows(Topic.Rows)
Rand Rand()

Table Examples

Function Example
Filter Filter(Topic.Items, Status = "Active")

ShowColumns ShowColumns(Topic.Items, "Id", "Name")
ForAll ForAll(Topic.Items, { Id: Id, Name: Name })
